q: A dental group is doubling in size over 18 months through acquisition. What happens to the cards and entity at each newly acquired clinic?
options: They're migrated to the group's brand and Xero entity within a week
They keep their old legacy entity and cards, creating an invisible second layer of spend
They're immediately closed and reissued under the holding company
Nothing changes because the group already uses one consolidated ledger
explanation: Acquired clinics keep their pre-acquisition entity and cards rather than switching to the group's brand — so head office inherits a layer of spend it can't see, on top of the layer it can.

q: At a rural and regional health group, why has the business effectively stopped issuing new corporate cards?
options: Head office caps the number of active cards at 50
Issuing a new card requires the CEO to physically fly to a bank branch to co-sign it in person
The bank stopped offering new cards to the industry
Staff prefer to use personal cards and get reimbursed
explanation: The debit-card-per-state-account setup means every new card needs the CEO's in-person co-signature at a branch — so painful that new cards essentially stopped being issued, leaving leavers' cards still active.

q: Inside one dental group, what's the real difference between its cleanest clinic and its messiest (recently acquired) clinic?
options: The messy clinic has fewer transactions but higher values
The clean clinic runs 15-20 well-documented transactions a month; the messy one runs 50-60 with barely any support and blurred personal/business spend
Both clinics run identical volumes but different currencies
The messy clinic simply doesn't use cards at all
explanation: Same company, wildly different site behaviour — 15-20 clean transactions versus 50-60 with capex mixed in with personal-looking purchases like a $70 honey jar.

q: What is the actual approval mechanism for card spend at a national radiology group?
options: A finance business partner signs off every transaction within 24 hours
CC'ing an external approver into an email — finance's only real lever is rejecting submissions missing that CC or a receipt
An automated workflow that blocks spend above $200 without pre-approval
A monthly board review of all card transactions
explanation: One finance leader said the approval process 'doesn't really exist' — CC'ing someone on an email is the whole mechanism, and rejection for a missing CC or receipt is an administrative gate, not a financial control.

q: At a large corporate healthcare group, why doesn't the existing card approval process (inside the bank's portal) actually prevent bad spend?
options: The portal doesn't support approvals at all
By the time anyone reviews it, the money has already been spent — it's a post-hoc formality
Approvals are outsourced to a third party who never responds
The bank charges extra to enable approvals
explanation: The finance exec admits 'it's already been spent anyway' by review time — which is why staff dodging a no-travel policy by booking flights on personal cards go undetected until after the fact.

q: Even using a 'free' bank card tool, how much finance time does a large corporate healthcare group lose each month chasing coding for just ONE business unit?
options: About half a day
About 3 finance-days
About 10 finance-days
None — the free tool fully automates coding
explanation: Roughly 3 finance-days a month for one business unit out of many — and that time cost alone is the business case for paying for a better tool.

q: An 8-practice GP group runs three separate legal entities and has deliberately chosen not to ___________ them into one Xero file because of the complexity involved.
hint: Think about merging multiple sets of books into a single ledger.
explanation: They deliberately avoided consolidating into one file — so any group-level view has to be manually stitched together across separate files and bank feeds.

q: At a national radiology group, staff are supposed to submit receipts within 48 hours, but in the finance controller's own words, 'at the moment it's a ___________... we're chasing them.'
hint: A one-word description of a workflow that's completely broken.
explanation: The finance controller called the receipt-chasing process a 'disaster' — the entire workflow rests on emailed photos and manual spreadsheet ticking.

q: A rural and regional health group's CFO described their AP approvals as 'blanket ___________ approvals' — invoices get rubber-stamped with no real scrutiny.
hint: The CFO's own blunt, informal word for approvals that aren't real approvals.
explanation: 'Blanket bullshit approvals' was the CFO's own description — invoices land in a shared inbox and get rubber-stamped 'approved, cheers' with the audit trail evaporating.

context: “We just hand the departing employee's card to whoever's covering for them — it's not a big deal.”
options: “That's fine, most businesses do that — let's move on.”
“How would you know today if three former employees still had an active card and a live subscription sitting on it?”
“You should really fire whoever set that process up.”
“Have you considered banning corporate cards altogether?”
explanation: Reframing to a concrete, unanswerable question about leaver risk surfaces the real exposure — untraceable subscriptions and shared credentials — without lecturing the prospect.

context: “Our staff know they need to keep receipts, so it mostly gets handled.”
options: “Great, then you won't need anything from us.”
“Realistically, what percentage of last month's transactions had a receipt attached and the right code on them by month-end?”
“You should fine staff who don't submit receipts on time.”
“Most businesses just accept that as a cost of doing business.”
explanation: Asking for the real number — not the intention — usually exposes the gap between policy and practice, which is the actual pain worth solving.

context: “We do have an approval process — everything gets reviewed in the bank's portal.”
options: “Perfect, that sounds like it's already solved.”
“Is that review happening before the money leaves the business, or after it's already been spent?”
“That portal is outdated, you should switch banks.”
“Approvals don't really matter for card spend anyway.”
explanation: This question draws the exact distinction most prospects haven't drawn themselves — between a real control (before spend) and compliance theatre (after spend).
